title: Pathways to Recovery for Pregnant & Parenting Women (PPW) in Northern Arizona: A Needs Assessment
keywords: health equity, policy, and population health
health care systems, delivery, and workforce innovation

summary.hook: This poster presents a needs assessment of the care ecosystem for pregnant and parenting women (PPW) with substance use disorder (SUD) in Northern Arizona, described as compassionate but structurally fragile.
summary.summary_30s: This poster presents a needs assessment of the care ecosystem for pregnant and parenting women (PPW) with substance use disorder (SUD) in Northern Arizona, described as compassionate but structurally fragile. Using semi-structured interviews across clinical, public health, justice, tribal leadership, and community perspectives, the authors identify fragmentation as the core system problem and emphasize that relationships (especially peers/paraprofessionals) are currently holding the system together. The poster proposes immediate, short-term, and long-term recommendations to standardize pathways, stabilize peer roles, improve coordination/data sharing, and align policy and payment structures.
talktrack_2m: Focus: Analyze: Identify strengths, gaps, and system-level opportunities for PPW care in Northern Arizona.
Approach: Population: Participants from clinical, public health, justice, tribal leadership, and community members. Primary outcome: Not stated on poster (qualitative themes across analytic domains).
Finding: The “core system problem” is fragmentation; relationships (not infrastructure) currently hold the system together.

Why it matters: Authors’ stated conclusions: Fragmentation is the core system problem; relationships (not infrastructure) currently hold the system together; sustainable change requires aligning people, policy, and payment. Implications (poster-grounded): Practice/operations: Standardize screening and warm handoffs (48–72 hours) and assign navigators/peers as single contact points. Workforce: Stabilize paraprofessional roles via pilot Medicaid reimbursement and expand training (PSI, MI, reflective supervision). Systems/policy: Develop HIPAA/data-sharing primers, resource directories, shared data platforms/intake hubs, bundled payments (pregnancy → 12 months postpartum), expand mother-and-baby residential capacity, and embed Indigenous leadership/ICWA-informed courts. Payer strategy: Tie payer pilots to avoided downstream costs (ED, NICU, foster care) and pursue payment parity (including audio-only visits). Mechanism/explanation (ONLY if stated): Not stated on poster (beyond the assertion that trust/relationships drive engagement and fragmentation undermines care). Connections to prior work (ONLY if referenced): Not stated on poster
key_numbers: The poster proposes immediate, short-term, and long-term recommendations to standardize pathways, stabilize peer roles, improve coordination/data sharing, and align policy and p...
takeaways: The “core system problem” is fragmentation; relationships (not infrastructure) currently hold the system together.
Housing, peers, and cultural responsiveness drive engagement more than any single clinical service.
Identification and engagement rely on trust and peer outreach; screening protocols are inconsistent; fear/stigma (e.g., fear of child removal) limits disclosure.
limitations: The poster does not list formal study limitations.
The poster leaves open this clarification: What recruitment strategy was used to identify the 22 participants, and what were the inclusion criteria for interviewees?

what_to_ask.question: In P5.T1, “identification relies on trust and peer outreach rather than formal screening”: what are the most common entry points you observed in practice?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This gets the presenter to interpret the main result instead of repeating the title.

what_to_ask.question: With n=22 across 17 interviews, how did you decide you had sufficient coverage of perspectives—did you assess thematic saturation? Not stated on poster.
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This probes confidence, assumptions, or limitations without sounding hostile.

what_to_ask.question: For the “Pregnant PPW Priority Pathway,” what would standardized screening look like in practice, and who would own it?
what_to_ask.why_this_is_good: This opens a useful conversation about why the work matters or where it goes next.

collaboration_ideas.idea: Clarify is the next study step—implementation planning, pilot testing the priority pathway, or evaluating a bundled payment model? Not stated on poster.

collaboration_ideas.what_they_get: A concrete role in shaping and evaluating the poster's next step.
